question: How does citympg differ across the various fueltype categories?

analysis.chart_type: Violin Plot with Individual Data Points Overlay
analysis.design_rationale: Violin plots show the kernel density estimation of citympg for each fueltype, giving a richer view of the distribution's shape (potential bimodality, skew, etc.), while overlaying individual data points offers insight into the spread and concentration. This unconventional chart highlights distributional differences, clustering of citympg values, and potential outlier structures more effectively than simple summary charts.
analysis.target_insight: This visualization reveals the full distribution of citympg for each fueltype, including density, central tendency, and spread, as well as the location of each individual car within those distributions.

chart_encodings.other_aesthetics: violin shape, alpha blending on scatter, points jittered for visibility

transformations: No aggregation; raw citympg values for each fueltype
Jitter individual points on x-axis for clarity
data_preparation_steps: Assign each car to its fueltype
Jitter individual points on x for scatter
suggestion.matplotlib_code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv("csv_c_squared\22.csv")
fueltypes = sorted(df["fueltype"].unique())
data_to_plot = [df[df["fueltype"] == ft]["citympg"].values for ft in fueltypes]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.violinplot(data_to_plot, positions=[0, 1], widths=0.6, showmeans=False, showmedians=True, showextrema=True)
colors = ["#006d2c", "#41b6c4"]
for i, ft in enumerate(fueltypes):
    y = df[df["fueltype"] == ft]["citympg"].values
    x = np.random.normal(loc=i, scale=0.08, size=len(y))
    ax.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.35, color=colors[i], edgecolor="none", s=40, label=ft)
ax.set_xticks([0, 1])
ax.set_xticklabels(fueltypes)
ax.set_xlabel("fueltype")
ax.set_ylabel("citympg")
ax.set_title("citympg Distribution and Raw Values by fueltype\n(Violin Plot + Points)")
plt.legend(title="fueltype", loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
